title: Oncology Endocrinopathies: Diagnostic Framework & Evidence-Based Management
subtitle: 20 Diagnostic Questions + 30 ASCO/ESMO Principles + 78 MCQs by Organ System
authors: User-provided clinical framework and 30 evidence-based management principles (chat); cross-checked against ASCO/ESMO-aligned literature including European Thyroid Journal ICI thyroid best practices (2025), Annual Review of Medicine (2020), Cleveland Clinic Journal of Medicine (2023), Endotext ICI endocrine irAE chapter, ICI-DM meta-analyses (Diabetic Medicine 2022; Frontiers Immunology 2024), and thyroid irAE prognosis meta-analyses
sourceFile: user-provided content (chat) — diagnostic questions and ASCO/ESMO-aligned management answers

items.title: Why must you differentiate ICIs, TKIs, cytotoxic chemotherapy, and hormone-suppressive therapies?
items.content:
Each oncological drug class carries a distinct endocrine side-effect profile.
Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) most commonly cause autoimmune endocrinopathies — thyroiditis, hypophysitis, adrenalitis, and insulin-dependent diabetes.
Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) such as sunitinib and sorafenib frequently cause primary hypothyroidism through a different mechanism.
Traditional cytotoxic chemotherapy and hormone-suppressive regimens (ADT, aromatase inhibitors) create yet another pattern dominated by gonadal suppression and bone loss.
Pinpointing the exact cause of an endocrine disorder in an oncology patient requires knowing which class is on board before ordering axis-specific workup.
keyPoints: ICIs → autoimmune endocrinopathies (thyroid, pituitary, adrenal, pancreas).
TKIs → frequent primary hypothyroidism (compare to baseline).
Cytotoxics and hormone-suppressive therapy → gonadal/bone effects dominate.
Drug class determines which of the 20 diagnostic questions matter most.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "You must differentiate between immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), traditional cytotoxic chemotherapy, or hormone-suppressive therapies, as each has distinct endocrine side-effect profiles."

items.title: How does the chronological timeline distinguish treatment-induced endocrinopathy?
items.content:
Establish when cancer therapy started and when endocrine symptoms first appeared.
ICI-induced endocrinopathies classically emerge in the 6–12 week window after initiation — hypophysitis with CTLA-4 inhibitors (e.g., ipilimumab) most often at 8–10 weeks — but onset can occur months after treatment has stopped.
PD-1/PD-L1–associated hypophysitis tends to present later (median ~24–40 weeks in some cohorts), so a "normal" first three months does not exclude immune-mediated pituitary disease.
A symptom that predates all oncologic therapy is unlikely to be treatment-induced unless it represents unmasked pre-existing disease.
keyPoints: Map symptom onset to therapy start/stop dates.
ICI endocrinopathies often appear 6–12 weeks in (especially CTLA-4 hypophysitis ~8–10 weeks).
Late and post-treatment onset is well documented.
PD-1/PD-L1 hypophysitis may present substantially later than CTLA-4.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "When did the cancer therapy initiate, and when did the endocrine symptoms first appear? For example, ICI-induced endocrinopathies typically emerge 6 to 12 weeks post-initiation, but can occur months after treatment has stopped."

items.title: Why can high-dose corticosteroid taper mimic a new endocrine disorder?
items.content:
Dexamethasone and other glucocorticoids used for chemotherapy premedication, brain metastases, or antiemesis suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis.
When steroids are tapered or stopped, the patient may develop fatigue, nausea, hypotension, and hyponatremia that look identical to ICI-induced adrenal insufficiency.
The key question is whether symptoms track steroid withdrawal rather than a new immune event.
Always reconcile the steroid timeline before attributing adrenal dysfunction solely to checkpoint inhibition.
keyPoints: High-dose steroids suppress the HPA axis.
Taper/withdrawal can cause secondary adrenal suppression.
Symptoms overlap with ICI-induced hypophysitis/adrenal insufficiency.
Steroid timeline must be mapped before blaming ICIs alone.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "If the patient received steroids (e.g., dexamethasone for chemotherapy premedication or brain metastases) that are now being tapered, are their symptoms actually due to secondary adrenal suppression or steroid withdrawal?"

items.title: How does radiation field determine endocrine risk?
items.content:
Localized radiation can directly injure endocrine organs depending on the field.
Brain/skull-base radiation risks hypopituitarism from pituitary or hypothalamic damage.
Head-and-neck radiation risks primary hypothyroidism and hypoparathyroidism.
Mediastinal radiation can affect thyroid tissue in the field.
Radiation-induced endocrine failure may present months to years after treatment — a delayed timeline that must be distinguished from acute ICI toxicity.
keyPoints: Brain/skull base → hypopituitarism risk.
Head and neck → hypothyroidism, hypoparathyroidism.
Mediastinum → thyroid in field.
Radiation effects may be delayed months to years.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "Determine if radiation fields included the brain/skull base (risk for hypopituitarism), the head and neck (risk for primary hypothyroidism or hypoparathyroidism), or the mediastinum."

items.title: Why is ICI-induced hypophysitis a life-threatening emergency?
items.content:
Hypophysitis — pituitary inflammation — is most strongly associated with CTLA-4 inhibitors such as ipilimumab, though it can occur with PD-1/PD-L1 agents.
Presenting features include severe or atypical headache, profound unexplained lethargy, nausea, dizziness, and hyponatremia.
At diagnosis, most patients have central adrenal insufficiency; central hypothyroidism and hypogonadism are also common.
Because ACTH deficiency can precipitate adrenal crisis, hypophysitis must be recognized and treated urgently — not dismissed as cancer-related fatigue.
keyPoints: Most common with CTLA-4 inhibitors (ipilimumab); also reported with PD-1/PD-L1.
Headache, lethargy, nausea, hyponatremia are key clues.
Central adrenal insufficiency present in most cases at diagnosis.
Adrenal crisis risk makes this an emergency, not a watch-and-wait finding.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Is the patient experiencing severe, atypical headaches, visual field deficits, or profound, unexplained lethargy that would suggest pituitary inflammation (most common with CTLA-4 inhibitors like ipilimumab)?"

items.title: How do you recognize impending adrenal crisis in an oncology patient?
items.content:
Adrenal crisis presents with unexplained orthostatic hypotension, refractory nausea and vomiting, extreme weakness, severe abdominal pain, confusion, and electrolyte abnormalities.
In the ICI setting, crisis may arise from primary adrenalitis (adrenal destruction) or secondary insufficiency from hypophysitis.
Any hemodynamically unstable oncology patient with nonspecific GI symptoms deserves an urgent 8:00 AM cortisol and ACTH before attributing symptoms to chemotherapy side effects or sepsis alone.
keyPoints: Orthostatic hypotension, refractory nausea/vomiting, weakness, abdominal pain.
Can arise from primary adrenalitis or secondary hypophysitis.
Do not attribute hemodynamic instability to chemo toxicity without adrenal labs.
Check morning cortisol and ACTH urgently.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Is the patient exhibiting unexplained orthostatic hypotension, refractory nausea and vomiting, extreme weakness, or severe abdominal pain?"

items.title: Why must cortisol and ACTH be interpreted together?
items.content:
Primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenalitis) shows low cortisol with inappropriately high ACTH as the pituitary attempts compensation.
Secondary adrenal insufficiency (hypophysitis) shows low cortisol with inappropriately low or normal ACTH because the pituitary cannot mount a response.
This distinction directs imaging (pituitary MRI for hypophysitis), predicts coexisting central hypothyroidism/hypogonadism, and determines whether mineralocorticoid replacement is needed (primary but not isolated secondary disease).
keyPoints: Primary AI: low cortisol + high ACTH.
Secondary AI (hypophysitis): low cortisol + low/normal ACTH.
Pattern guides pituitary imaging and hormone replacement strategy.
Mineralocorticoid replacement needed in primary, not typical secondary AI.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Have you checked these to distinguish between primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenalitis, indicated by low cortisol and high ACTH) and secondary adrenal insufficiency (hypophysitis, indicated by low cortisol and inappropriately low/normal ACTH)?"

items.title: Why must adrenal insufficiency be ruled out or treated before starting thyroid hormone?
items.content:
Central hypothyroidism from hypophysitis often coexists with secondary adrenal insufficiency.
Levothyroxine increases metabolic demand; if cortisol is deficient, thyroid replacement can unmask or precipitate cardiovascular collapse and fatal adrenal crisis.
The rule is straightforward: treat or empirically cover adrenal insufficiency before initiating thyroid hormone replacement when central hypothyroidism is suspected in an ICI-treated patient.
keyPoints: Central hypothyroidism and secondary AI frequently coexist in hypophysitis.
Thyroid hormone raises metabolic demand and cortisol requirement.
Untreated AI + levothyroxine can precipitate fatal adrenal crisis.
Cover or treat adrenal insufficiency before starting thyroid replacement.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "If central hypothyroidism is suspected, have you ensured that adrenal insufficiency was ruled out or treated first? Initiating thyroid hormone replacement in a patient with untreated adrenal insufficiency can precipitate a fatal adrenal crisis."

items.title: How does a biphasic thyroid response signal ICI-induced destructive thyroiditis?
items.content:
The hallmark of ICI-related thyroid dysfunction is destructive thyroiditis: stored hormone release causes a brief thyrotoxic phase (palpitations, anxiety, weight loss — often mild or asymptomatic), followed by crash into hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain).
Many patients ultimately require long-term levothyroxine because the hypothyroid phase is often permanent.
This pattern differs from TKI-associated hypothyroidism, which typically presents as gradual primary hypothyroidism without a preceding hyperthyroid phase.
keyPoints: Destructive thyroiditis: transient thyrotoxicosis → hypothyroidism.
Thyrotoxic phase may be brief or asymptomatic.
Hypothyroid phase often permanent; levothyroxine commonly needed.
Distinct from gradual TKI-induced primary hypothyroidism.
items.reference: III. The Thyroid Axis: "Did the patient experience brief symptoms of hyperthyroidism (palpitations, anxiety, weight loss) followed by a crash into hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain)? This destructive thyroiditis pattern is the hallmark of ICI-induced thyroid dysfunction."

items.title: Why compare current thyroid labs to pre-treatment baseline on TKIs?
items.content:
TKIs such as sunitinib and sorafenib frequently cause primary hypothyroidism through mechanisms distinct from ICI autoimmune thyroiditis.
A rising TSH with falling free T4 compared to the patient's own pre-treatment baseline strongly supports TKI effect rather than unrelated primary thyroid disease.
Baseline thyroid function should be documented before starting TKI therapy whenever possible so that on-treatment shifts are interpretable.
keyPoints: Sunitinib and sorafenib commonly cause hypothyroidism.
Compare on-treatment TSH/free T4 to pre-TKI baseline.
Rising TSH from personal baseline supports drug effect.
Mechanism differs from ICI destructive thyroiditis.
items.reference: III. The Thyroid Axis: "If the patient is on a TKI (like sunitinib or sorafenib, which frequently cause hypothyroidism), how do their current TSH and Free T4 levels compare to their pre-treatment baseline?"

items.title: When do compressive neck symptoms redirect the differential away from pure drug toxicity?
items.content:
Goiter, neck tenderness, or dysphagia raise concern for direct tumor invasion of the thyroid or adjacent structures, radiation-induced fibrosis, or (rarely) painful subacute thyroiditis.
These localizing symptoms warrant neck imaging and may indicate structural disease rather than a purely systemic immune or TKI effect.
Absence of compressive symptoms supports a biochemical endocrinopathy workup as the primary pathway.
keyPoints: Goiter, neck tenderness, dysphagia → structural differential.
Consider tumor invasion, post-radiation change, painful thyroiditis.
Imaging may be required when localizing symptoms present.
Pure ICI thyroiditis is usually painless.
items.reference: III. The Thyroid Axis: "Does the patient have a goiter, neck tenderness, or dysphagia that might indicate direct tumor invasion, radiation-induced changes, or (rarely) painful thyroiditis?"

items.title: Why is sudden polyuria and polydipsia a red flag on ICIs?
items.content:
Abrupt onset of excessive thirst and urination in a patient on immune checkpoint inhibitors should trigger immediate evaluation for new-onset insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus from autoimmune islet destruction.
Unlike gradual hyperglycemia in type 2 diabetes, ICI-associated diabetes can present explosively and may be the first sign of pancreatic autoimmunity.
Capillary glucose, venous glucose, and ketone assessment should not be delayed.
keyPoints: Sudden polyuria/polydipsia on ICIs → evaluate for new diabetes immediately.
Mechanism: autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells.
Presentation can be abrupt, unlike slow T2DM progression.
Check glucose and ketones without delay.
items.reference: IV. The Pancreatic & Metabolic Axes: "Is the patient suddenly excessively thirsty and urinating frequently, which are red flags for ICI-induced insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus?"

items.title: How does ICI-induced diabetes often present as diabetic ketoacidosis?
items.content:
ICI-related diabetes frequently resembles new-onset type 1 diabetes with absolute insulin deficiency.
Patients may present with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) — high capillary glucose, ketonuria or ketonemia, acidosis — as the inaugural event rather than mild asymptomatic hyperglycemia.
Always check ketones when evaluating unexpected hyperglycemia in an ICI-treated patient; DKA requires urgent insulin and fluid resuscitation.
keyPoints: ICI diabetes can present as abrupt DKA.
Check capillary glucose and urine/blood ketones.
Resembles new-onset type 1–like insulin deficiency.
DKA requires urgent insulin and volume resuscitation.
items.reference: IV. The Pancreatic & Metabolic Axes: "What is the patient's current capillary blood glucose, and are there ketones present in the urine or blood? ICI-induced diabetes often presents abruptly as DKA."

items.title: Why distinguish de novo type 1–like diabetes from worsening type 2 on steroids or cancer stress?
items.content:
Known type 2 diabetes may worsen from glucocorticoids, infection, or metabolic stress without implying ICI-induced autoimmunity.
De novo insulin-dependent diabetes with low or undetectable C-peptide and positive autoantibodies in a previously nondiabetic patient strongly supports ICI-mediated islet destruction.
The distinction matters for lifelong insulin dependence, immunotherapy continuation decisions, and avoiding mislabeling steroid-induced hyperglycemia as a permanent autoimmune endocrinopathy.
keyPoints: T2DM can worsen from steroids, stress, infection — not necessarily ICI autoimmunity.
De novo insulin dependence in a previously nondiabetic patient is the red flag.
C-peptide and autoantibodies help separate mechanisms.
Guides long-term insulin need and irAE attribution.
items.reference: IV. The Pancreatic & Metabolic Axes: "Does the patient have a known history of Type 2 Diabetes that is simply poorly controlled due to cancer stress/steroids, or is this a completely new onset of pancreatic islet cell destruction?"

items.title: What systemic effects follow androgen deprivation or aromatase inhibitor therapy?
items.content:
Androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) for prostate cancer and aromatase inhibitors for breast cancer intentionally suppress sex-steroid production.
Expected consequences include vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes), severe loss of libido, mood lability, and fatigue.
These are pharmacologic effects of the oncology regimen rather than unexpected immune toxicity — but they must still be distinguished from depression, disease progression, or unrelated endocrine disorders.
keyPoints: ADT and aromatase inhibitors intentionally suppress sex steroids.
Hot flashes, libido loss, mood lability are expected.
Distinguish pharmacologic effect from progression or unrelated disease.
Counsel and treat symptomatically when appropriate.
items.reference: V. Gonadal, Bone, and Parathyroid Health: "If the patient is on Androgen Deprivation Therapy (ADT) for prostate cancer or aromatase inhibitors for breast cancer, are they experiencing new-onset vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes), severe loss of libido, or mood lability?"

items.title: Why monitor bone mineral density during hormone-suppressive oncology regimens?
items.content:
ADT and aromatase inhibitors accelerate bone loss by lowering circulating androgens or estrogens.
Fragility fractures or a DEXA scan showing rapid decline in bone mineral density may be directly linked to the hormone-suppressive regimen rather than unrelated osteoporosis.
Baseline and serial bone density assessment, calcium/vitamin D optimization, and antiresorptive therapy should be considered per oncology/endocrine guidelines when risk is high.
keyPoints: Hormone-suppressive therapy accelerates bone loss.
Fragility fractures or falling DEXA T-scores may be treatment-related.
Not every fracture in a cancer patient is from metastatic disease.
Prophylactic bone protection may be indicated.
items.reference: V. Gonadal, Bone, and Parathyroid Health: "Has the patient experienced any fragility fractures, or does a recent DEXA scan show accelerated bone loss directly linked to their hormone-suppressive oncology regimen?"

items.title: How do abnormal calcium levels confound the endocrine picture in oncology?
items.content:
Hypocalcemia with muscle cramping or tetany after head-and-neck radiation suggests hypoparathyroidism.
Hypercalcemia with confusion or constipation may reflect hyperparathyroidism — but in oncology patients, malignancy-related hypercalcemia from bone metastases or PTHrP secretion is far more common and can mimic a primary parathyroid disorder.
Always interpret calcium in the context of malignancy burden, PTH level, and radiation history.
keyPoints: Post-neck radiation hypocalcemia → consider hypoparathyroidism.
Hypercalcemia in cancer → bone mets/PTHrP more likely than primary hyperparathyroidism.
Check PTH and malignancy context.
Do not assume every calcium abnormality is a primary endocrine disorder.
items.reference: V. Gonadal, Bone, and Parathyroid Health: "Are there signs of hypocalcemia (muscle cramping, tetany) suggesting hypoparathyroidism post-neck radiation, or hypercalcemia (confusion, constipation) that could indicate parathyroid issues or, more likely, bone metastases mimicking an endocrine disorder?"

items.title: Why does pre-existing autoimmunity increase suspicion for ICI endocrinopathy?
items.content:
Personal or family history of autoimmune disease — Hashimoto thyroiditis, vitiligo, Addison disease, type 1 diabetes — may predate oncology treatment.
ICIs can unmask or aggravate underlying autoimmunity rather than create an entirely de novo process.
A patient with pre-existing thyroid autoantibodies, for example, is at higher risk for ICI thyroid dysfunction; baseline autoantibody status helps interpret new biochemical abnormalities.
keyPoints: Pre-existing autoimmunity may be unmasked or worsened by ICIs.
Hashimoto, vitiligo, Addison, T1DM history matters.
Thyroid autoantibodies predict higher ICI thyroid irAE risk.
Baseline autoimmunity status aids attribution.
items.reference: VI. Confounding Factors & Baselines: "Is there a personal or family history of autoimmune diseases (e.g., Hashimoto's thyroiditis, vitiligo, Addison's disease) that pre-dated the oncological treatment and has simply been unmasked or aggravated?"

items.title: How can cancer progression mimic endocrine disease?
items.content:
Extreme fatigue, anorexia, and weight loss — symptoms common to many endocrinopathies — are also cardinal features of cancer cachexia, anemia, and tumor progression.
Before launching a full endocrine panel, assess whether the clinical picture tracks rising tumor burden, falling albumin, new metastases, or declining performance status.
Treatment-attributable endocrinopathy and disease progression can coexist; the framework requires parallel evaluation, not either-or thinking.
keyPoints: Fatigue, anorexia, weight loss occur in both endocrinopathy and progression.
Assess tumor burden, cachexia, anemia, performance status.
Do not assume every constitutional symptom is an irAE.
Progression and endocrinopathy can coexist.
items.reference: VI. Confounding Factors & Baselines: "Could the patient's current 'endocrine' symptoms—such as extreme fatigue, anorexia, and weight loss—actually be driven by cancer cachexia, anemia, or tumor progression?"

items.title: Which non-oncology medications commonly confound endocrine evaluation?
items.content:
Amiodarone can cause both hypothyroidism and thyrotoxicosis.
Opioids suppress the gonadal and adrenal axes and may lower testosterone and cortisol responses.
Glucocorticoids, as discussed, cause HPA suppression.
A complete medication reconciliation — including over-the-counter supplements — is mandatory before attributing laboratory abnormalities to cancer therapy alone.
keyPoints: Amiodarone → thyroid dysfunction.
Opioids → suppressed gonadal and adrenal axes.
Glucocorticoids → HPA axis suppression.
Full medication reconciliation required for attribution.
items.reference: VI. Confounding Factors & Baselines: "Is the patient taking other drugs known to alter endocrine function (e.g., amiodarone affecting the thyroid, or opioids suppressing the gonadal and adrenal axes) that are confounding the clinical picture?"

items.title: Why are visual field deficits a weaker clue for ICI hypophysitis than the source suggests?
items.content:
The original framework lists visual field deficits among hypophysitis clues.
Independent review of ICI hypophysitis cohorts finds visual field changes are rare — unlike classic sellar mass compression from other causes of hypophysitis.
ICI hypophysitis more often presents with headache, fatigue, nausea, and hyponatremia without chiasmal compression.
Clinicians should not withhold pituitary-axis testing because the visual fields are normal.
keyPoints: Source lists visual field deficits; literature reports them as rare in ICI hypophysitis.
Headache, fatigue, nausea, hyponatremia are more reliable clues.
Normal visual fields do not exclude hypophysitis.
Pituitary-axis labs remain indicated with compatible symptoms.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Is the patient experiencing severe, atypical headaches, visual field deficits, or profound, unexplained lethargy that would suggest pituitary inflammation (most common with CTLA-4 inhibitors like ipilimumab)?" — cross-checked: Cleveland Clinic Journal of Medicine (2023) notes ICI hypophysitis is "usually not accompanied by visual disturbances."

items.question: A 58-year-old man with metastatic melanoma started ipilimumab 9 weeks ago. He presents with severe headache, profound fatigue, and nausea. Morning labs: cortisol 2.1 µg/dL (low), ACTH 8 pg/mL (inappropriately low). TSH is also low. What is the most likely diagnosis and immediate priority?
options: Primary adrenal insufficiency from metastatic adrenal invasion; start fludrocortisone
ICI-induced hypophysitis with secondary adrenal insufficiency; glucocorticoid replacement before thyroid hormone
Euthyroid sick syndrome from cancer cachexia; observe
Steroid withdrawal from prior dexamethasone premedication; resume high-dose steroids indefinitely
items.explanation: Low cortisol with inappropriately low ACTH at ~9 weeks on ipilimumab is classic secondary adrenal insufficiency from hypophysitis. CTLA-4 hypophysitis typically presents around 8–10 weeks. Low TSH suggests coexisting central hypothyroidism. Glucocorticoids must be started before levothyroxine to avoid adrenal crisis. Primary AI would show high ACTH; cachexia does not explain the ACTH pattern; steroid withdrawal is possible but less likely without a recent taper history matching the timeline.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Have you checked these to distinguish between primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenalitis, indicated by low cortisol and high ACTH) and secondary adrenal insufficiency (hypophysitis, indicated by low cortisol and inappropriately low/normal ACTH)?"

items.question: A woman on combined ipilimumab and nivolumab has confirmed central hypothyroidism (low free T4, low TSH). Morning cortisol is 3 µg/dL and ACTH is suppressed. She is hemodynamically stable. What is the correct next step before starting levothyroxine?
options: Start levothyroxine immediately because she is euthyroid on cortisol alone
Initiate physiologic glucocorticoid replacement, then start levothyroxine
Start levothyroxine and add hydrocortisone only if hypotension develops
Stop all ICIs permanently and observe without hormone replacement
items.explanation: Untreated adrenal insufficiency plus thyroid hormone replacement can precipitate fatal adrenal crisis. Glucocorticoid replacement must precede levothyroxine when central hypothyroidism and secondary AI coexist — a common hypophysitis pattern. Waiting for hypotension is unsafe; ICIs may often continue once hormones are replaced, but adrenal coverage cannot be deferred.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Initiating thyroid hormone replacement in a patient with untreated adrenal insufficiency can precipitate a fatal adrenal crisis."

items.question: A 65-year-old with NSCLC on pembrolizumab had brief palpitations and anxiety at week 6; at week 10 she reports fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance. TSH is now elevated with low free T4. Thyroid uptake scan shows low uptake. What pattern does this represent?
options: Destructive ICI thyroiditis with transient thyrotoxicosis followed by hypothyroidism
items.explanation: Brief hyperthyroid symptoms followed by hypothyroidism with low uptake is the hallmark destructive thyroiditis pattern of ICI thyroid dysfunction. Graves would show sustained hyperthyroidism with high uptake. Central hypothyroidism shows low TSH, not elevated TSH. Amiodarone is not in the vignette.
items.reference: III. The Thyroid Axis: "Did the patient experience brief symptoms of hyperthyroidism (palpitations, anxiety, weight loss) followed by a crash into hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain)? This destructive thyroiditis pattern is the hallmark of ICI-induced thyroid dysfunction."

items.question: A 52-year-old without prior diabetes started nivolumab 8 weeks ago. He presents with polyuria, polydipsia, glucose 486 mg/dL, and positive urine ketones. Which statement is most accurate?
options: This is expected steroid-induced hyperglycemia; hold ICIs and observe
This likely represents ICI-induced insulin-dependent diabetes and requires urgent insulin for possible DKA
Oral metformin alone is sufficient; ketones are a benign finding
This confirms pre-existing type 2 diabetes; no relation to immunotherapy
items.explanation: Sudden polyuria/polydipsia with hyperglycemia and ketones in a previously nondiabetic ICI patient is classic for autoimmune beta-cell destruction presenting as DKA. This requires urgent insulin and volume resuscitation, not observation or metformin monotherapy. Attribution to pre-existing T2DM is incorrect without prior diabetes history.
items.reference: IV. The Pancreatic & Metabolic Axes: "ICI-induced diabetes often presents abruptly as DKA."

items.question: Morning labs in an ICI-treated patient with hypotension and vomiting: cortisol 1.8 µg/dL, ACTH 112 pg/mL (elevated). What is the most likely mechanism?
options: Primary adrenal insufficiency from ICI-related adrenalitis
items.explanation: Low cortisol with elevated ACTH indicates primary adrenal insufficiency — the adrenal gland cannot produce cortisol and the pituitary compensates with high ACTH. Secondary hypophysitis shows low/normal ACTH. Factitious Cushing would suppress ACTH. This is pathologic, not a normal stress response.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenalitis, indicated by low cortisol and high ACTH)"

items.question: A patient with brain metastases received dexamethasone 8 mg daily for 6 weeks, now tapered off over 2 weeks. Two weeks after the last dose she has fatigue, nausea, and BP 92/58 mmHg. ICI was never started. Best explanation?
options: Secondary adrenal suppression from recent high-dose glucocorticoid taper
items.explanation: High-dose glucocorticoids suppress the HPA axis; symptoms after taper/withdrawal mimic adrenal insufficiency and must be distinguished from ICI toxicity. No ICI was given, making hypophysitis unlikely. The timeline tracks steroid withdrawal, not parathyroid or thyroid disease.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "If the patient received steroids (e.g., dexamethasone for chemotherapy premedication or brain metastases) that are now being tapered, are their symptoms actually due to secondary adrenal suppression or steroid withdrawal?"

items.question: A patient on sunitinib for RCC had pre-treatment TSH 1.8 mIU/L. After 3 months on drug, TSH is 9.4 mIU/L with low-normal free T4. She denies neck pain or goiter. Most likely cause?
options: TKI-associated primary hypothyroidism compared to baseline
items.explanation: Rising TSH from personal baseline on sunitinib supports TKI-induced primary hypothyroidism — a frequent TKI effect. No ICI is involved. TSH-secreting adenoma would cause high free T4. Painful thyroiditis is inconsistent with the painless presentation.
items.reference: III. The Thyroid Axis: "If the patient is on a TKI (like sunitinib or sorafenib, which frequently cause hypothyroidism), how do their current TSH and Free T4 levels compare to their pre-treatment baseline?"

items.question: A patient treated for laryngeal cancer with neck radiation 2 years ago presents with perioral numbness, carpopedal spasm, and calcium 7.1 mg/dL. PTH is low. Most likely diagnosis?
options: Hypercalcemia of malignancy from bone metastases
items.explanation: Hypocalcemia with low PTH after head-and-neck radiation strongly suggests hypoparathyroidism from parathyroid gland injury. Hypercalcemia of malignancy and primary hyperparathyroidism present with high calcium. ICI hypophysitis is not suggested by isolated hypocalcemia with low PTH years after neck radiation.
items.reference: V. Gonadal, Bone, and Parathyroid Health: "Are there signs of hypocalcemia (muscle cramping, tetany) suggesting hypoparathyroidism post-neck radiation"

items.question: A patient on pembrolizumab has progressive fatigue and 8-kg weight loss over 6 weeks. CT shows new liver metastases. TSH and cortisol are normal. What is the most appropriate conclusion?
options: Normal TSH and cortisol exclude any clinical problem
Symptoms may primarily reflect disease progression/cachexia; endocrine labs do not exclude coexisting irAE
Stop pembrolizumab permanently without further evaluation
items.explanation: Constitutional symptoms with new metastases may reflect progression and cachexia rather than endocrinopathy. Normal screening labs do not exclude all irAEs (e.g., evolving thyroiditis, intermittent AI). Empiric thyroid hormone without indication is unsafe; stopping ICI requires individualized assessment, not automatic reflex.
items.reference: VI. Confounding Factors & Baselines: "Could the patient's current 'endocrine' symptoms—such as extreme fatigue, anorexia, and weight loss—actually be driven by cancer cachexia, anemia, or tumor progression?"

items.question: When evaluating whether a new endocrine problem is treatment-related in an oncology patient, which initial step is most foundational?
options: Map the exact drug classes, treatment start/stop dates, and symptom onset timeline
Obtain DEXA scan before any laboratory testing
items.explanation: The framework begins with treatment history and timeline — differentiating ICIs, TKIs, cytotoxics, hormone suppression, steroids, and radiation fields — because each class predicts distinct endocrine risks and typical onset windows. Random single tests or imaging without timeline context cannot attribute causality.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "What exact oncological agents is the patient receiving?" and "What is the precise chronological timeline?"

items.title: Why is thyroid dysfunction far more common with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors than with CTLA-4 alone?
items.content:
Among immune checkpoint inhibitors, thyroid irAEs are significantly more frequent with PD-1 and PD-L1 blockade (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, atezolizumab) than with CTLA-4 monotherapy (ipilimumab).
Meta-analyses show hypothyroidism and destructive thyroiditis clustering on anti–PD-1/PD-L1 regimens, whereas ipilimumab dominates the hypophysitis spectrum instead.
Agent-specific risk profiling prevents misattributing thyroid labs to the wrong drug when combination regimens are used.
keyPoints: Thyroid irAEs predominate on PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors.
CTLA-4 inhibitors (ipilimumab) are more tied to hypophysitis than thyroid disease.
Combination ICI regimens carry additive endocrine risk across axes.
Always map labs to the specific agents on board.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Thyroid dysfunction is significantly more common with PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab, nivolumab) compared to CTLA-4 inhibitors (e.g., ipilimumab)."

items.title: How do TKIs such as sunitinib and lenvatinib cause hypothyroidism?
items.content:
Tyrosine kinase inhibitors cause hypothyroidism through mechanisms distinct from ICI autoimmune thyroiditis.
Proposed pathways include ischemic/destructive injury to thyroid follicular cells and altered peripheral metabolism of thyroid hormones.
The result is gradual primary hypothyroidism — typically without a preceding thyrotoxic phase — making baseline and on-treatment TSH/free T4 comparison essential.
keyPoints: TKI hypothyroidism ≠ ICI destructive thyroiditis mechanism.
Ischemic gland injury and altered hormone metabolism are proposed pathways.
Sunitinib and lenvatinib are frequent offenders.
Gradual primary hypothyroidism without a thyrotoxic prelude is typical.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (TKIs) like sunitinib and lenvatinib cause hypothyroidism through ischemic destruction of the thyroid gland and altered metabolism of thyroid hormones."

items.title: Why is ICI-induced hypothyroidism usually permanent?
items.content:
Unlike several other immune-related adverse events that may resolve after ICI discontinuation, ICI-induced hypothyroidism following destructive thyroiditis is almost exclusively permanent.
The hypothyroid phase reflects irreversible follicular destruction rather than reversible thyroiditis like classic subacute thyroiditis.
Patients should be counseled that lifelong levothyroxine replacement is the expected outcome once persistent hypothyroidism is confirmed.
keyPoints: Post-thyroiditis hypothyroid phase is usually permanent on ICIs.
Unlike some irAEs, thyroid function rarely normalizes off drug.
Lifelong levothyroxine is the standard expectation.
Counsel early to avoid false hope of spontaneous recovery.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Unlike many other immune-related adverse events (irAEs), ICI-induced hypothyroidism is almost exclusively permanent and requires lifelong levothyroxine replacement."

items.title: Why do guidelines avoid high-dose corticosteroids for mild ICI thyrotoxicosis?
items.content:
During the transient thyrotoxic phase of ICI destructive thyroiditis, symptoms are often mild or asymptomatic.
Current ASCO/ESMO-aligned guidance favors symptomatic management with beta-blockers (e.g., propranolol, atenolol) rather than high-dose corticosteroids, which are not shown to prevent progression to hypothyroidism and carry their own toxicities.
Reserve immunosuppressive steroid doses for severe, symptomatic thyrotoxicosis or other irAEs with clear steroid-responsive mass effect.
keyPoints: Thyrotoxic phase is often mild/asymptomatic.
Beta-blockers for symptomatic relief — not routine high-dose steroids.
Steroids do not reliably prevent permanent hypothyroidism.
Match steroid use to severity and specific irAE type.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Guidelines advise against the use of high-dose corticosteroids for asymptomatic or mild ICI-induced thyrotoxicosis, favoring symptomatic management with beta-blockers instead."

items.title: How should grade 1–2 immune-related thyroid dysfunction affect ICI continuation?
items.content:
Mild to moderate immune-related thyroid dysfunction (CTCAE grade 1 or 2) does not mandate cessation of checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
Oncology and endocrine guidelines support continuing ICIs concurrently with levothyroxine replacement once hypothyroidism is confirmed, provided the patient is clinically stable.
Permanent ICI discontinuation is reserved for life-threatening or refractory irAEs — not routine thyroid replacement scenarios.
keyPoints: Grade 1–2 thyroid irAEs: continue ICIs in most cases.
Concurrent levothyroxine replacement is standard.
Do not reflexively stop immunotherapy for manageable hypothyroidism.
Reserve ICI hold for severe/life-threatening endocrine events.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Mild to moderate immune-related thyroid dysfunction (Grade 1 or 2) does not require the cessation of oncological therapy; checkpoint inhibitors can be continued concurrently with hormone replacement."

items.title: How often should TSH and free T4 be monitored during ICI therapy?
items.content:
Evidence-based protocols mandate thyroid surveillance every 4 to 6 weeks during active ICI treatment.
This interval captures the asymptomatic thyrotoxic phase of destructive thyroiditis before patients crash into hypothyroidism.
Symptom-triggered testing alone misses subclinical transitions that affect both endocrine management and attribution of fatigue to cancer versus irAE.
keyPoints: Monitor TSH and free T4 every 4–6 weeks on ICIs.
Detects asymptomatic thyrotoxicosis before hypothyroid transition.
Do not rely on symptoms alone.
Early detection allows timely beta-blocker or levothyroxine initiation.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Evidence-based protocols mandate monitoring Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH) and Free T4 every 4 to 6 weeks during ICI therapy to identify asymptomatic dysfunction early."

items.subtopic: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: Why is hypophysitis distinctively linked to CTLA-4 inhibitors?
items.content:
Autoimmune pituitary inflammation (hypophysitis) has a strong, distinct association with CTLA-4 inhibitors — particularly ipilimumab — with incidence far exceeding that seen with PD-1/PD-L1 monotherapy.
Older male patients appear disproportionately affected in many cohorts.
When hypophysitis is suspected, identify whether CTLA-4 blockade (alone or in combination) is in the regimen, because this drives pre-test probability and urgency of pituitary-axis testing.
keyPoints: Hypophysitis is strongly linked to CTLA-4 inhibitors (ipilimumab).
Much rarer with PD-1/PD-L1 monotherapy.
Older males overrepresented in many series.
Combination ipilimumab + anti–PD-1 raises overall endocrine risk.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "Autoimmune inflammation of the pituitary gland (hypophysitis) has a strong, distinct association with CTLA-4 inhibitors, particularly presenting in older male patients."

items.subtopic: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: How does ICI hypophysitis typically present, and when do visual symptoms matter?
items.content:
Classic hypophysitis symptoms include severe headaches, profound fatigue, and — when pituitary edema compresses the optic chiasm — visual field defects, typically 6 to 12 weeks after CTLA-4–based treatment initiation.
However, many ICI hypophysitis cases present without visual disturbances; headache, nausea, hyponatremia, and hypotension from adrenal insufficiency may dominate.
MRI and urgent axis labs should not be withheld when visual fields are normal but the clinical timeline and agent profile fit hypophysitis.
keyPoints: Classic triad: headache, fatigue, visual field loss from chiasmal compression.
Typical onset 6–12 weeks on CTLA-4 regimens.
Visual field defects occur but are not universal in ICI hypophysitis.
Normal fields do not exclude hypophysitis — pursue axis labs and MRI.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "Classic symptoms include severe headaches, profound fatigue, and visual field defects due to pituitary edema, typically occurring 6 to 12 weeks after treatment initiation."

items.subtopic: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: Why is ACTH deficiency from ICI hypophysitis almost always permanent?
items.content:
Central adrenal insufficiency from ICI-induced hypophysitis is usually permanent — corticotroph destruction rarely reverses, unlike some other pituitary axes.
Unrecognized ACTH deficiency is potentially fatal during intercurrent illness, surgery, or if levothyroxine is started before glucocorticoid coverage.
All affected patients require lifelong hydrocortisone with explicit stress-dosing and sick-day education.
keyPoints: ACTH/cortisol deficiency from hypophysitis is usually permanent (86–100% in series).
Recovery of secondary AI is extremely rare.
Fatal if unrecognized — especially before thyroid replacement.
Lifelong hydrocortisone + stress-dose protocols mandatory.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "Central adrenal insufficiency resulting from ICI-induced hypophysitis is usually permanent and potentially fatal if unrecognized, requiring lifelong hydrocortisone stress-dosing protocols."

items.subtopic: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: How reversible are the non-adrenal pituitary axes after ICI hypophysitis?
items.content:
While ACTH deficiency is usually permanent, central hypothyroidism and hypogonadism from hypophysitis may recover in a substantial minority — reported in up to 50–60% of patients over several months in some cohorts, though recovery rates vary (thyroid axis recovery ~24% in at least one strict irH series; gonadal ~58%).
Guidelines recommend periodic re-evaluation of TSH/free T4 and gonadal axes every 3–6 months in year one to detect recovery and avoid unnecessary overtreatment.
Do not assume all pituitary deficiencies share the same permanence as ACTH.
keyPoints: ACTH axis: usually permanent.
TSH and gonadal axes: recovery possible in up to ~50–60% (cohort-dependent).
Re-evaluate every 3–6 months in year one.
Titrate or stop replacement if axes recover.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "While the ACTH axis rarely recovers, central hypothyroidism and hypogonadism caused by hypophysitis reverse in 50% to 60% of patients over several months."

items.subtopic: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: Why are high-dose corticosteroids reserved for acute hypophysitis with mass effect?
items.content:
High-dose corticosteroids during acute ICI hypophysitis are indicated when the patient has severe mass-effect symptoms — particularly optic chiasm compression with visual field loss — not for every case of biochemically confirmed hypopituitarism.
Steroids may reduce pituitary edema acutely but do not reliably restore permanent ACTH deficiency.
Long-term management still requires physiologic hormone replacement regardless of initial steroid use.
keyPoints: High-dose steroids for severe mass effect (e.g., chiasmal compression).
Not indicated for all biochemically confirmed hypopituitarism.
Steroids may shrink acute edema but rarely restore ACTH production.
Chronic physiologic replacement remains the mainstay.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "High-dose corticosteroids are indicated during the acute phase of hypophysitis only if the patient exhibits severe mass-effect symptoms, such as optic chiasm compression."

items.subtopic: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: How is primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenalitis) distinguished from hypophysitis?
items.content:
Autoimmune adrenalitis is a rare but life-threatening ICI irAE affecting the adrenal gland directly.
Laboratory signature: low morning cortisol with markedly elevated ACTH plus electrolyte derangements — hyponatremia and hyperkalemia — from mineralocorticoid deficiency.
Hypophysitis shows low cortisol with inappropriately low/normal ACTH and typically normal potassium; both require urgent glucocorticoid replacement but primary AI also needs fludrocortisone.
keyPoints: Primary adrenalitis: low cortisol + high ACTH.
Electrolytes: hyponatremia + hyperkalemia suggest primary AI.
Hypophysitis: low cortisol + low/normal ACTH.
Primary AI requires fludrocortisone; secondary AI does not.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "Autoimmune adrenalitis is a rare but life-threatening irAE, differentiated from hypophysitis by low morning cortisol combined with markedly elevated ACTH and electrolyte derangements (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia)."

items.subtopic: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: Why must empiric IV hydrocortisone precede diagnostic confirmation in suspected adrenal crisis?
items.content:
Any oncology patient on immunotherapy presenting with refractory nausea, severe abdominal pain, and unexplained hypotension must receive immediate empiric intravenous hydrocortisone before waiting for confirmatory ACTH stimulation testing.
Diagnostic delay during adrenal crisis increases mortality; blood should be drawn for cortisol and ACTH first, but treatment must not wait for results.
This applies equally to primary adrenalitis and secondary hypophysitis presentations.
keyPoints: Refractory nausea + abdominal pain + hypotension → treat first, confirm second.
Draw cortisol/ACTH before dosing, but do not delay IV hydrocortisone.
Adrenal crisis mortality rises with treatment delay.
Applies to both primary and secondary adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "Any oncology patient on immunotherapy presenting with refractory nausea, severe abdominal pain, and unexplained hypotension must receive immediate empiric intravenous hydrocortisone prior to diagnostic confirmation."

items.subtopic: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: How does cranial radiotherapy cause delayed hypopituitarism?
items.content:
High-dose cranial radiotherapy carries a dose-dependent risk of hypopituitarism that may manifest years or even decades after treatment concludes.
Growth hormone and gonadotropin deficiencies often appear first; ACTH and TSH deficiency may follow progressively.
Survivors of childhood or adult brain/CNS tumors require long-term pituitary surveillance independent of current ICI exposure.
keyPoints: Cranial RT → dose-dependent hypopituitarism risk.
Onset may be delayed years to decades post-treatment.
GH and gonadotropin deficiencies often precede corticotroph/thyrotroph failure.
Long-term surveillance required in RT survivors.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "High-dose cranial radiotherapy carries a dose-dependent risk of delayed hypopituitarism, which can manifest years or even decades after oncological treatment concludes."

items.subtopic: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: Why does ICI-induced diabetes resemble abrupt type 1 diabetes?
items.content:
PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibitors can trigger rapid-onset, insulin-dependent diabetes through autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells.
Presentation is often fulminant in previously nondiabetic patients, distinct from slow worsening of type 2 diabetes under cancer stress.
All ICI-treated patients with new polyuria, polydipsia, or unexplained hyperglycemia need urgent glucose and ketone assessment.
keyPoints: PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors can cause autoimmune beta-cell destruction.
Rapid insulin dependence in previously nondiabetic patients.
Distinct from steroid-exacerbated type 2 diabetes.
Urgent glucose + ketone testing for new symptoms.
items.reference: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders: "ICIs, specifically PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibitors, can trigger rapid-onset, insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus through autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells."

items.subtopic: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: How often does ICI-induced diabetes present as DKA?
items.content:
Diabetic ketoacidosis is the initial presentation in a large majority of ICI-induced diabetes cases — approximately 68–76% in major cohorts and meta-analyses, consistent with the source figure of up to 70%.
This high DKA rate reflects absolute insulin deficiency developing acutely rather than gradually compensated hyperglycemia.
Oncology teams should maintain low threshold for ketone testing in symptomatic hyperglycemia on ICIs.
keyPoints: ~68–76% present with DKA initially (source cites up to 70%).
Reflects abrupt absolute insulin deficiency.
Often first manifestation — not preceded by long asymptomatic hyperglycemia.
Ketone testing mandatory for symptomatic ICI patients.
items.reference: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders: "Up to 70% of patients with ICI-induced diabetes present initially in Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), requiring emergent inpatient management."

items.subtopic: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: What biochemical profile defines ICI-induced insulin-dependent diabetes?
items.content:
ICI-DM is characterized by severely low or undetectable C-peptide, confirming absolute beta-cell failure and insulin dependence.
Unlike classic type 1 diabetes where anti-GAD and related autoantibodies are frequently positive, ICI-DM shows a mixed autoantibody profile — many patients are antibody-negative, but approximately half may have at least one positive pancreatic autoantibody in larger series.
Low C-peptide is the most reliable marker of permanent insulin requirement.
keyPoints: Hallmark: severely low/undetectable C-peptide.
Confirms absolute insulin deficiency.
Autoantibody positivity is variable (~50% in some cohorts).
Do not exclude ICI-DM because anti-GAD is negative.
items.reference: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders: "This distinct phenotype of diabetes is characterized by severely low or undetectable C-peptide levels, but typically lacks traditional autoantibodies (like anti-GAD) found in classic Type 1 diabetes."

items.title: Why is the 'antibody-negative' ICI-DM phenotype overstated in the source?
items.content:
The original answer states ICI-DM typically lacks traditional autoantibodies such as anti-GAD.
Independent verification: a systematic review/meta-analysis found ketoacidosis in 76% of cases and that approximately half of patients had at least one positive pancreatic autoantibody at presentation; antibody-positive cases had faster onset and higher DKA rates.
Clinically, low C-peptide defines insulin dependence — not antibody status — and both antibody-positive and antibody-negative phenotypes occur.
keyPoints: Source: 'typically lacks' anti-GAD and related antibodies.
Verified: ~50% may have ≥1 positive pancreatic autoantibody.
C-peptide, not antibody status, defines insulin dependence.
Test autoantibodies but do not require them for diagnosis.
items.reference: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders: "typically lacks traditional autoantibodies (like anti-GAD) found in classic Type 1 diabetes" — cross-checked: Stamatouli et al. meta-analysis (Diabetic Medicine 2022) reports ~50% antibody-positive at presentation.

items.subtopic: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: Why is ICI-induced beta-cell destruction irreversible?
items.content:
Once ICI-mediated pancreatic islet destruction occurs, beta-cell function does not recover — immunosuppression has not been shown to reverse established ICI-DM.
All affected patients require lifelong basal-bolus insulin therapy with standard type 1 diabetes sick-day and DKA-prevention education.
Glucocorticoids used for other irAEs may worsen glycemic control but do not restore endogenous insulin secretion.
keyPoints: Beta-cell destruction is permanent.
Immunosuppression does not reverse established ICI-DM.
Lifelong basal-bolus insulin required.
Standard T1DM education applies (sick-day rules, DKA prevention).
items.reference: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders: "ICI-induced beta-cell destruction is permanent; affected patients will require lifelong basal-bolus insulin therapy, and immunosuppression does not reverse the condition."

items.subtopic: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: How does glucocorticoid-induced hyperglycemia differ from ICI-DM?
items.content:
High-dose steroids for chemotherapy premedication or management of other irAEs frequently exacerbate pre-existing type 2 diabetes or induce secondary hyperglycemia.
Unlike ICI-DM, steroid-induced hyperglycemia often improves as steroids are tapered, and C-peptide is preserved.
Always map the steroid timeline and C-peptide level before labeling every hyperglycemic ICI patient as permanent autoimmune diabetes.
keyPoints: Steroids worsen T2DM or cause new hyperglycemia.
Often reverses with steroid taper.
C-peptide typically preserved — unlike ICI-DM.
Timeline and C-peptide distinguish mechanisms.
items.reference: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders: "High-dose steroids used for chemotherapy premedication or managing other irAEs frequently exacerbate pre-existing Type 2 diabetes or induce secondary diabetes, which may reverse upon steroid taper."

items.subtopic: IV. Bone, Calcium & Gonadal Health — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: Why does ADT cause rapid bone loss in prostate cancer?
items.content:
Androgen deprivation therapy drastically accelerates bone resorption by removing androgen-mediated osteoblast support.
Osteopenia and fragility fracture risk increase significantly within the first year of ADT — not only after years of treatment.
Baseline DEXA and fracture risk assessment should occur at ADT initiation, not deferred until symptomatic fracture.
keyPoints: ADT accelerates bone resorption rapidly.
Fragility fracture risk rises within the first year.
Baseline DEXA at ADT start is guideline-supported.
Distinguish treatment-related bone loss from metastatic bone disease.
items.reference: IV. Bone, Calcium, & Gonadal Health: "Androgen Deprivation Therapy (ADT) for prostate cancer drastically accelerates bone resorption, causing osteopenia and significantly increasing fragility fracture risk within the first year of use."

items.subtopic: IV. Bone, Calcium & Gonadal Health — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: How do aromatase inhibitors affect bone mineral density?
items.content:
Aromatase inhibitors for breast cancer induce profound estrogen depletion that accelerates bone mineral density loss at a rate exceeding physiological menopause.
This is an expected pharmacologic consequence, not a sign of occult metastatic bone disease.
Fracture prevention strategies should be discussed at treatment initiation for patients with elevated baseline fracture risk.
keyPoints: AIs cause profound estrogen depletion.
BMD loss faster than natural menopause.
Expected pharmacologic effect of therapy.
Proactive bone protection in high-risk patients.
items.reference: IV. Bone, Calcium, & Gonadal Health: "Aromatase inhibitors used in breast cancer induce profound estrogen depletion, accelerating bone mineral density loss at a rate higher than physiological menopause."

items.subtopic: IV. Bone, Calcium & Gonadal Health — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: Why are anti-resorptive agents recommended during prolonged ADT or aromatase inhibitor therapy?
items.content:
Clinical guidelines strongly recommend prophylactic anti-resorptive therapy — bisphosphonates or denosumab — to prevent skeletal-related events in patients on prolonged ADT or aromatase inhibitors who have elevated fracture risk.
This is primary prevention of treatment-induced osteoporosis, distinct from treating established bone metastases.
Agent selection balances renal function (bisphosphonates), rebound fracture risk on denosumab discontinuation, and calcium/vitamin D repletion requirements.
keyPoints: Guidelines support bisphosphonates or denosumab for ADT/AI bone protection.
Primary prevention of treatment-induced osteoporosis.
Distinct from bone metastasis management.
Denosumab requires careful discontinuation planning.
items.reference: IV. Bone, Calcium, & Gonadal Health: "Clinical guidelines strongly recommend anti-resorptive agents (bisphosphonates or denosumab) to prevent skeletal-related events in patients undergoing prolonged ADT or aromatase inhibitor therapy."

items.subtopic: IV. Bone, Calcium & Gonadal Health — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: How is hypercalcemia of malignancy most often mediated?
items.content:
Hypercalcemia of malignancy (HCM) is a primary oncologic emergency most frequently driven by tumor secretion of parathyroid hormone-related protein (PTHrP) — humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy — rather than purely osteolytic metastases alone.
Solid tumors such as squamous cell lung cancer, renal cell carcinoma, and breast cancer are classic PTHrP secretors.
Recognizing HCM prevents misdiagnosis as primary hyperparathyroidism and directs oncology-directed treatment.
keyPoints: HCM is an oncologic emergency.
PTHrP-mediated humoral mechanism is most common.
Osteolytic metastases are a separate but coexisting pathway.
Do not confuse with primary hyperparathyroidism.
items.reference: IV. Bone, Calcium, & Gonadal Health: "HCM is a primary oncologic emergency most frequently mediated by tumor secretion of Parathyroid Hormone-related Protein (PTHrP) rather than direct osteolytic metastases."

items.subtopic: IV. Bone, Calcium & Gonadal Health — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: Why is suppressed PTH the diagnostic hallmark separating HCM from primary hyperparathyroidism?
items.content:
In hypercalcemia of malignancy, intact PTH is suppressed by negative feedback from high calcium — the parathyroid glands are appropriately quiescent.
Primary hyperparathyroidism shows inappropriately normal or elevated PTH despite hypercalcemia.
Measuring intact PTH is the single most important step when evaluating hypercalcemia in a cancer patient.
keyPoints: HCM: high calcium + suppressed PTH.
Primary HPT: high calcium + normal/elevated PTH.
PTH is the key first-line discriminator.
Guides whether oncology vs parathyroid surgery workup is needed.
items.reference: IV. Bone, Calcium, & Gonadal Health: "The diagnostic hallmark separating Hypercalcemia of Malignancy from Primary Hyperparathyroidism is a suppressed intact Parathyroid Hormone (PTH) level."

items.subtopic: IV. Bone, Calcium & Gonadal Health — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: Why must calcium and vitamin D be replete before denosumab or zoledronic acid?
items.content:
Denosumab and zoledronic acid used for bone metastases or osteoporosis prophylaxis can cause severe, life-threatening hypocalcemia — especially in patients with vitamin D deficiency or impaired renal calcium handling.
Guidelines mandate baseline correction of calcium and vitamin D before administration and ongoing monitoring during therapy.
Symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, QT prolongation, seizures) is preventable with proper pre-treatment repletion.
keyPoints: Denosumab and zoledronic acid → hypocalcemia risk.
Correct calcium and vitamin D before first dose.
Monitor calcium during therapy.
Hypocalcemia can be severe and life-threatening if unprevented.
items.reference: IV. Bone, Calcium, & Gonadal Health: "Denosumab and zoledronic acid, utilized for bone metastases, can cause severe, life-threatening hypocalcemia, mandating baseline correction of calcium and Vitamin D prior to administration."

items.subtopic: IV. Bone, Calcium & Gonadal Health — Evidence-Based Management
items.title: How is radiation-induced hypoparathyroidism managed differently from simple calcium supplementation?
items.content:
Head, neck, or mediastinal radiotherapy can permanently destroy parathyroid tissue, causing PTH-deficient hypocalcemia that is often refractory to oral calcium alone.
Activated vitamin D analogs (calcitriol) are required because absent PTH cannot convert 25-OH vitamin D to active 1,25-OH form at the kidney.
Monitor calcium, phosphate, and renal function long-term; hypoparathyroidism may present years after radiation.
keyPoints: Post-neck/mediastinal RT → permanent hypoparathyroidism possible.
Low PTH → refractory hypocalcemia.
Calcitriol (activated vitamin D) required — not calcium alone.
Delayed presentation years after RT is documented.
items.reference: IV. Bone, Calcium, & Gonadal Health: "Localized radiotherapy to the head, neck, or mediastinum can cause permanent hypoparathyroidism, leading to refractory hypocalcemia requiring activated Vitamin D (calcitriol) analogs."

items.title: Why does physiologic hormone replacement not reduce ICI anti-tumor efficacy?
items.content:
Treating immunotherapy-induced endocrinopathies with physiologic hormone replacement — levothyroxine, hydrocortisone, insulin — does not diminish the anti-tumor efficacy of checkpoint inhibitors.
Clinicians should replace deficient hormones promptly without fear that correction will blunt cancer response.
This principle supports continuing ICIs alongside replacement for grade 1–2 endocrine irAEs when clinically appropriate.
keyPoints: Physiologic replacement does not reduce ICI efficacy.
Replace deficient hormones promptly.
Do not withhold levothyroxine/hydrocortisone/insulin out of efficacy concerns.
Supports continuing ICIs with concurrent replacement.
items.reference: V. Global Management & Efficacy: "Treating immunotherapy-induced endocrinopathies with physiologic hormone replacement does not diminish the anti-tumor efficacy of the checkpoint inhibitors."

items.title: How is development of endocrine irAEs correlated with ICI outcomes?
items.content:
Emerging clinical evidence — including multiple meta-analyses — suggests that endocrine irAEs, particularly thyroid dysfunction, are associated with improved progression-free and overall survival across several malignancies.
A large meta-analysis found thyroid irAE associated with OS HR ~0.52 and PFS HR ~0.58 versus no thyroid irAE.
This is interpreted as a marker of robust immune activation rather than a reason to withhold endocrine treatment; correlation does not prove causation and immortal time bias must be considered in observational data.
keyPoints: Thyroid/endocrine irAEs correlate with better PFS/OS in multiple studies.
Meta-analysis: thyroid irAE OS HR ~0.52 (95% CI 0.43–0.62).
Likely reflects effective immune activation, not direct hormonal benefit.
Observational data — interpret with immortal time bias caution.
items.reference: V. Global Management & Efficacy: "Emerging clinical evidence suggests that the development of endocrine irAEs (particularly thyroiditis) is positively correlated with improved progression-free and overall survival in several malignancies."

items.question: A 62-year-old on pembrolizumab for NSCLC develops asymptomatic low TSH with elevated free T4 at week 8. She has mild palpitations. Per ASCO/ESMO-aligned guidance, what is the best initial management?
options: Propranolol for symptomatic relief; avoid high-dose corticosteroids; continue pembrolizumab
items.explanation: Asymptomatic or mild ICI thyrotoxicosis during the destructive thyroiditis phase is managed with beta-blockers, not high-dose corticosteroids. ICIs can continue for grade 1–2 thyroid irAEs. Steroids do not prevent progression to hypothyroidism. RAI and permanent ICI stop are not indicated.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Guidelines advise against the use of high-dose corticosteroids for asymptomatic or mild ICI-induced thyrotoxicosis, favoring symptomatic management with beta-blockers instead."

items.question: A patient on nivolumab develops grade 2 hypothyroidism requiring levothyroxine. TSH is stabilizing on replacement. What is the recommended approach to immunotherapy?
options: Hold nivolumab until TSH normalizes off levothyroxine
Add high-dose prednisone to restore thyroid function
items.explanation: Grade 1–2 thyroid dysfunction does not require ICI cessation. Continue checkpoint inhibitors with concurrent levothyroxine. ICI hypothyroidism is usually permanent — waiting for normalization off replacement is inappropriate. Steroids do not restore destroyed thyroid tissue.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Mild to moderate immune-related thyroid dysfunction (Grade 1 or 2) does not require the cessation of oncological therapy; checkpoint inhibitors can be continued concurrently with hormone replacement."

items.subtopic: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes — Evidence-Based Management
items.question: An ICI-treated patient has cortisol 1.5 µg/dL, ACTH 186 pg/mL (elevated), sodium 128 mEq/L, potassium 5.8 mEq/L. What is the diagnosis and required replacement beyond hydrocortisone?
options: Primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenalitis); hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone
items.explanation: Low cortisol with markedly elevated ACTH plus hyponatremia and hyperkalemia indicates primary adrenal insufficiency from adrenalitis. Mineralocorticoid deficiency requires fludrocortisone in addition to hydrocortisone. Secondary hypophysitis shows low/normal ACTH and typically normal potassium.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "differentiated from hypophysitis by low morning cortisol combined with markedly elevated ACTH and electrolyte derangements (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia)."

items.subtopic: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes — Evidence-Based Management
items.question: A patient on ipilimumab presents with vomiting, abdominal pain, BP 78/50 mmHg. Blood is drawn for cortisol and ACTH. What must happen next within minutes?
options: Wait for ACTH stimulation test results before any treatment
Immediate empiric IV hydrocortisone while awaiting labs
Start levothyroxine first to improve hemodynamics
Obtain pituitary MRI before any intervention
items.explanation: Suspected adrenal crisis in an ICI patient requires empiric IV hydrocortisone immediately — draw labs first but never delay treatment for confirmation. Levothyroxine before glucocorticoid coverage can be fatal. MRI is important but not before resuscitation.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "must receive immediate empiric intravenous hydrocortisone prior to diagnostic confirmation."

items.subtopic: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders — Evidence-Based Management
items.question: A previously nondiabetic patient on pembrolizumab presents with DKA. C-peptide is undetectable; anti-GAD is negative. What is the most accurate conclusion?
options: This excludes ICI-induced diabetes because antibodies are negative
This confirms ICI-induced insulin-dependent diabetes; lifelong insulin is required regardless of antibody status
Oral metformin alone will suffice after DKA resolves
items.explanation: ICI-DM is defined by absolute insulin deficiency (undetectable C-peptide), not by autoantibody positivity — approximately half of cases may have positive antibodies, half negative. Lifelong insulin is required; immunosuppression does not reverse beta-cell destruction. Metformin monotherapy is insufficient.
items.reference: III. Pancreatic & Metabolic Disorders: "severely low or undetectable C-peptide levels" and "ICI-induced beta-cell destruction is permanent."

items.subtopic: IV. Bone, Calcium & Gonadal Health — Evidence-Based Management
items.question: A patient with metastatic squamous lung cancer has calcium 13.2 mg/dL, PTH 8 pg/mL (suppressed), and no bisphosphonate yet. What is the most likely mechanism?
options: Hypercalcemia of malignancy with PTHrP secretion
Vitamin D intoxication with elevated PTH
items.explanation: Hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH in an active cancer patient is classic hypercalcemia of malignancy — most often PTHrP-mediated in squamous cell cancers. Primary HPT shows elevated or inappropriately normal PTH. FHH and vitamin D toxicity do not fit this suppressed-PTH oncology picture.
items.reference: IV. Bone, Calcium, & Gonadal Health: "HCM is a primary oncologic emergency most frequently mediated by tumor secretion of Parathyroid Hormone-related Protein (PTHrP)" and "suppressed intact Parathyroid Hormone (PTH) level."

items.subtopic: IV. Bone, Calcium & Gonadal Health — Evidence-Based Management
items.question: A patient with bone metastases is about to receive denosumab. Calcium is 8.4 mg/dL and 25-OH vitamin D is 14 ng/mL. What is required before the first injection?
options: Proceed immediately — denosumab corrects calcium automatically
Replete calcium and vitamin D to adequate levels before administration
Start calcitriol only after the first fracture occurs
Avoid all bone agents and use opioids instead
items.explanation: Denosumab and zoledronic acid can cause severe hypocalcemia, especially with pre-existing vitamin D deficiency. Guidelines mandate correcting calcium and vitamin D before administration. Denosumab does not auto-correct calcium; delaying repletion risks tetany and cardiac complications.
items.reference: IV. Bone, Calcium, & Gonadal Health: "mandating baseline correction of calcium and Vitamin D prior to administration."

items.question: A patient on nivolumab develops hypothyroidism and is started on levothyroxine. The oncology team worries that hormone replacement will reduce immunotherapy benefit. What does current evidence support?
options: Withhold levothyroxine to preserve anti-tumor efficacy
Physiologic hormone replacement does not diminish ICI anti-tumor efficacy — treat the endocrinopathy
Stop nivolumab permanently once any irAE occurs
Use supraphysiologic levothyroxine to boost immunity
items.explanation: Physiologic hormone replacement for ICI endocrinopathies does not reduce checkpoint inhibitor efficacy. Prompt treatment is safe and recommended. Withholding replacement harms the patient without oncologic benefit. Supraphysiologic dosing is not indicated.
items.reference: V. Global Management & Efficacy: "Treating immunotherapy-induced endocrinopathies with physiologic hormone replacement does not diminish the anti-tumor efficacy of the checkpoint inhibitors."

items.question: What is the classic clinical progression of ICI-induced biphasic thyroiditis?
options: Gradual onset of primary hypothyroidism over years
Transient thyrotoxicosis followed by rapid-onset, permanent hypothyroidism
Transient hypothyroidism followed by spontaneous recovery
items.explanation: Immune checkpoint inhibitors frequently induce a destructive, biphasic thyroiditis characterized by transient thyrotoxicosis followed by rapid-onset, permanent hypothyroidism.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) frequently induce a destructive, biphasic thyroiditis characterized by transient thyrotoxicosis followed by rapid-onset, permanent hypothyroidism."

items.question: What is the underlying mechanism of the transient thyrotoxicosis seen in ICI therapy?
options: Increased iodine uptake by the gland
items.explanation: The condition is a destructive thyroiditis — transient thyrotoxicosis is caused by release of preformed hormones from gland destruction, not hyperfunction.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "destructive biphasic thyroiditis characterized by transient thyrotoxicosis"

items.question: Which class of immune checkpoint inhibitors carries a significantly higher risk of inducing thyroid dysfunction?
options: PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab, nivolumab)
items.explanation: Thyroid dysfunction is significantly more common with PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibitors compared to CTLA-4 inhibitors.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Thyroid dysfunction is significantly more common with PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab, nivolumab) compared to CTLA-4 inhibitors (e.g., ipilimumab)."

items.question: When comparing immune-related adverse events (irAEs), how does the thyroid risk of ipilimumab compare to nivolumab?
options: Ipilimumab has a higher risk of thyroid dysfunction.
Ipilimumab has a lower risk of thyroid dysfunction.
Both have an equal risk of thyroid dysfunction.
items.explanation: Ipilimumab is a CTLA-4 inhibitor with lower thyroid dysfunction risk compared to the PD-1 inhibitor nivolumab.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Thyroid dysfunction is significantly more common with PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibitors compared to CTLA-4 inhibitors."

items.question: How do Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (TKIs) like sunitinib cause hypothyroidism?
options: By direct suppression of pituitary TSH
Through ischemic destruction of the gland and altered hormone metabolism
By blocking iodine absorption in the gut
items.explanation: TKIs cause hypothyroidism through ischemic destruction of the thyroid gland and altered metabolism of thyroid hormones.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (TKIs) like sunitinib and lenvatinib cause hypothyroidism through ischemic destruction of the thyroid gland and altered metabolism of thyroid hormones."

items.question: Which of the following oncology drugs are most classically associated with TKI-induced hypothyroidism?
items.explanation: Sunitinib and lenvatinib are specifically identified as TKIs that cause hypothyroidism.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (TKIs) like sunitinib and lenvatinib cause hypothyroidism"

items.question: Regarding the reversibility of ICI-induced hypothyroidism, which statement is true?
options: It reverses in 50% of patients upon stopping therapy.
It is highly responsive to high-dose corticosteroids.
It usually transitions back to euthyroidism after 6 months.
items.explanation: Unlike many other irAEs, ICI-induced hypothyroidism is almost exclusively permanent.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Unlike many other immune-related adverse events (irAEs), ICI-induced hypothyroidism is almost exclusively permanent and requires lifelong levothyroxine replacement."

items.question: What is the required long-term management for a patient who develops ICI-induced hypothyroidism?
items.explanation: Because the condition is permanent, affected patients require lifelong levothyroxine replacement.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "requires lifelong levothyroxine replacement."

items.question: A patient on pembrolizumab develops asymptomatic, mild ICI-induced thyrotoxicosis. What is the recommended management according to guidelines?
options: Avoid corticosteroids and manage symptomatically if needed
items.explanation: Guidelines advise against high-dose corticosteroids for asymptomatic or mild ICI-induced thyrotoxicosis.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Guidelines advise against the use of high-dose corticosteroids for asymptomatic or mild ICI-induced thyrotoxicosis, favoring symptomatic management with beta-blockers instead."

items.question: If a patient experiences symptoms from mild ICI-induced thyrotoxicosis (such as palpitations), what is the preferred pharmacological intervention?
items.explanation: Symptomatic management with beta-blockers is favored over corticosteroids for mild thyrotoxicosis.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "favoring symptomatic management with beta-blockers instead."

items.question: Does a patient with Grade 2 immune-related thyroid dysfunction need to permanently stop their checkpoint inhibitor?
options: Yes, to prevent life-threatening thyroid storm
Yes, until the thyroid completely recovers
No, checkpoint inhibitors can be continued concurrently with hormone replacement
No, but the dose of the ICI must be reduced by 50%
items.explanation: Mild to moderate immune-related thyroid dysfunction (Grade 1 or 2) does not require cessation of oncological therapy.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Mild to moderate immune-related thyroid dysfunction (Grade 1 or 2) does not require the cessation of oncological therapy; checkpoint inhibitors can be continued concurrently with hormone replacement."

items.question: A patient develops Grade 1 hypothyroidism on nivolumab. What is the next best step in oncology management?
options: Switch to a CTLA-4 inhibitor, monitor TSH
Add a high-dose steroid, continue nivolumab
items.explanation: The checkpoint inhibitor can be continued concurrently with levothyroxine replacement.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "checkpoint inhibitors can be continued concurrently with hormone replacement."

items.question: How frequently do evidence-based protocols mandate monitoring TSH and Free T4 during active ICI therapy?
items.explanation: Protocols mandate monitoring TSH and Free T4 every 4 to 6 weeks during ICI therapy.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "Evidence-based protocols mandate monitoring Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH) and Free T4 every 4 to 6 weeks during ICI therapy to identify asymptomatic dysfunction early."

items.question: What is the primary clinical rationale for routine 4-to-6-week thyroid laboratory monitoring during ICI therapy?
options: To decide when to initiate immunosuppression
To adjust the dose of the oncology drug
items.explanation: The goal of routine monitoring every 4 to 6 weeks is to identify asymptomatic dysfunction early.
items.reference: I. Thyroid Dysfunction: "every 4 to 6 weeks during ICI therapy to identify asymptomatic dysfunction early."

items.question: Which specific class of immunotherapies has a strong, distinct association with autoimmune hypophysitis?
items.explanation: Autoimmune inflammation of the pituitary gland (hypophysitis) has a strong, distinct association with CTLA-4 inhibitors.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "Autoimmune inflammation of the pituitary gland (hypophysitis) has a strong, distinct association with CTLA-4 inhibitors, particularly presenting in older male patients."

items.question: Which patient demographic most classically presents with CTLA-4 inhibitor-induced hypophysitis?
items.explanation: Hypophysitis particularly presents in older male patients on CTLA-4 inhibitors.
items.reference: II. Pituitary & Adrenal Axes: "particularly presenting in older male patients."
